title: Chana masala
subtitle: Spiced chickpea curry with tomato and garam masala
excerpt: Chickpeas simmered in a thick sauce of caramelised onion, ginger, garlic, tomato, and spices. A Punjabi curry-house staple and one of the most popular vegetarian dishes on the British Indian menu. The chickpeas are cooked until they absorb the sauce and the whole dish becomes deep and fragrant. Served with rice or bhatura (fried bread).
sourceNotes: Chana masala (chole masala) is a Punjabi dish eaten across Northern India and Pakistan, brought into the British curry-house tradition in the 1970s. It is documented in Madhur Jaffrey's cookbooks and is one of the most commonly ordered vegetarian dishes in British Indian restaurants.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Better the next day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make 2 days ahead. Flavours develop on standing.

content.text: Tinned chickpeas work perfectly here and make this a very quick dish. The key step is mashing roughly 20% of the chickpeas once they're in the sauce — this thickens the curry and gives it a denser body that raw chickpeas don't provide. Press the back of the spoon against the chickpeas in the pan a few times rather than using a masher.

The spice blend for chana masala is heavier on cumin and coriander than a standard curry base, and dried mango powder (amchoor) gives the characteristic slight sourness. Without amchoor, a small squeeze of lemon juice at the end achieves a similar effect. Either approach works; the tanginess is the point.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a wide pan over medium-high heat. Add the {{cumin-seeds}} and fry for 30 seconds until they pop. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 12 to 15 minutes until deep golden. Add the {{garlic}} and {{ginger-root}} and cook for 2 minutes. Add the {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, {{chilli-powder}}, and {{turmeric}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and cook for 8 to 10 minutes until reduced to a thick paste.

Add the {{chickpeas-tinned}} and 150 ml water. Stir well to coat in the sauce. Simmer over medium heat for 15 minutes. Using the back of a spoon, roughly crush about a quarter of the chickpeas against the side of the pan to thicken the sauce. Stir in the {{garam-masala}} and season with salt and a squeeze of lemon juice if using. The sauce should be thick and clinging to the chickpeas.

items.cause: Not enough reduction before or after adding chickpeas
items.fix: Simmer uncovered and stir frequently. Crush more chickpeas against the pan to release their starch and thicken the sauce
Taste after 15 minutes and adjust salt. The chickpeas need time to absorb the spiced sauce — don't rush this stage
Add lemon juice cautiously — it should brighten without dominating. If too sharp, add a pinch of sugar to balance

items.cause: Heat too high after chickpeas added
items.fix: Reduce the heat to medium after adding chickpeas. Stir frequently and add a splash of water if sticking

content.text: Chana masala is the most ordered vegetarian curry on the British Indian menu, eaten both as a main with rice and as a side dish alongside meat curries. In Punjab it is traditionally served with bhatura (deep-fried leavened bread) as chole bhature, a combination particularly popular as a breakfast or lunch. The British curry-house version is typically drier and more tomato-forward than the Punjabi original, which can use tamarind and dried mango for its sour note.
